meta.name: Daily Reflection Tree
meta.description: An end-of-day deterministic reflection tool across three psychological axes: Locus of Control, Orientation (Contribution vs Entitlement), and Radius of Concern (Self vs Others).

nodes.text: Good evening. You made it to the end of another day. Before you close the chapter on today — let's look at it together. This will take about 5 minutes. No right answers. Just honesty.
If today were a weather report, what would it say?
options: Clear skies — things went mostly my way
Cloudy with patches of sun — mixed but manageable
Heavy rain — rough and draining
Storm I didn't see coming — completely caught off guard

match: Clear skies — things went mostly my way
Cloudy with patches of sun — mixed but manageable
Heavy rain — rough and draining
Storm I didn't see coming — completely caught off guard

nodes.text: You described today as "{A1_OPEN.answer}". When something went well, what was the main reason?
options: I had prepared and it showed
I stayed flexible when the plan changed
The team delivered and I was part of that
I got a bit lucky, honestly

nodes.text: You described today as "{A1_OPEN.answer}". When things got difficult, what was your instinct?
options: Find the one thing I could actually change
Wait it out — some things just need time
Look for who could fix it faster than me
Push through on my own, quietly

match: I had prepared and it showed
I stayed flexible when the plan changed
The team delivered and I was part of that
I got a bit lucky, honestly
Find the one thing I could actually change
Wait it out — some things just need time
Look for who could fix it faster than me
Push through on my own, quietly

nodes.text: Think of one specific moment today where you made a decision — even a small one. What drove it?
options: I saw a gap and chose to fill it
I decided to say something instead of staying quiet
I chose to ask for help rather than struggle alone
I deliberately stepped back to let someone else lead

nodes.text: Looking back — was there a moment today where the outcome was more in your hands than you initially thought?
options: Yes — I realize now I had more say than I claimed
Somewhat — I could have influenced it but didn't
Not really — the conditions were genuinely against me
I'm not sure yet

nodes.text: On a tough day, it's natural to feel pulled by forces outside you. But somewhere in today, you made at least one small call. What was it closest to?
options: Choosing to stay calm when I could have escalated
Deciding to keep going when I wanted to stop
Choosing not to say something I would have regretted
I genuinely can't identify a choice I made today

nodes.text: You're operating with agency. You noticed where you had a choice and you made one — that's not small. Most people narrate their day as something that happened *to* them. You described yours as something you *moved through*. Hold onto that.

There's honesty in recognizing that your hand was in the outcome — even partially. That small noticing is where growth lives. Today may have felt collaborative or fortunate, but you were in it too. Don't disappear from your own story.

Rough days pull attention outward — to what others did or didn't do, to what went wrong beyond your reach. That's a fair response to a hard day. But you were still in there, making micro-decisions. The question isn't whether you had full control. It's whether you noticed where you had *any*.
Okay. You've looked at how you *navigated* today. Now let's look at what you *gave*.
Think of your most significant interaction today — a meeting, a conversation, a task shared with someone. What's the first thing that comes to mind about it?
options: I gave more than was expected of me
I felt my effort wasn't acknowledged
I helped someone past a block they were stuck on
I was doing my part — nothing more, nothing less

match: I gave more than was expected of me
I helped someone past a block they were stuck on
I felt my effort wasn't acknowledged
I was doing my part — nothing more, nothing less

nodes.text: You mentioned giving or helping. What was the nature of that contribution?
options: I covered something that wasn't technically my job
I gave someone time when I didn't really have it
I shared knowledge or a skill freely
I made a decision that reduced stress for my team

nodes.text: That's worth sitting with. When you think about today's effort, what feels most true?
options: I did good work and no one seemed to notice
Others got credit for effort I put in too
I kept things running but it felt invisible
I'm not sure I gave my best today, honestly

nodes.text: Did you need any recognition for what you gave today, or was the giving enough?
options: The giving was enough — I wasn't thinking about credit
I didn't expect recognition, but it would have been nice
I hoped someone would notice, at least quietly
Honestly, I did want acknowledgment and didn't get it

nodes.text: Think of just one moment today where you could have given something — time, attention, effort — but didn't. What held you back?
options: I was genuinely too stretched to give more
I didn't think it was my responsibility
I didn't notice the opportunity in the moment
I noticed but chose to protect my own bandwidth

match: The giving was enough — I wasn't thinking about credit
I didn't expect recognition, but it would have been nice
I hoped someone would notice, at least quietly
Honestly, I did want acknowledgment and didn't get it
I was genuinely too stretched to give more
I didn't think it was my responsibility
I didn't notice the opportunity in the moment
I noticed but chose to protect my own bandwidth

nodes.text: Giving without keeping score — that's rare. Most people carry a mental ledger: I gave this, so I should get that. When you give freely, you change the emotional tone of the space around you without even realizing it. That's not nothing. That's actually how cultures shift.

You gave today — and you also wanted to be seen for it. Both are true. Wanting recognition isn't entitlement; it's human. The question is whether the desire for recognition shapes what you give next time. Notice the difference between 'I gave and want credit' and 'I'll only give if I get credit.'

Being stretched is real — and protecting yourself from burnout is not selfishness. But it's worth noticing the difference between 'I couldn't give more' and 'I didn't want to.' One is a resource constraint. The other is a choice. Tomorrow, one moment to give something small — not because it's required, but because you can.

The gap between 'what I deserve' and 'what I give' is often invisible to the person holding it. You've just made it visible. That's not easy. The path from entitlement to contribution isn't a moral lecture — it's just noticing that giving freely tends to be its own reward in ways that credit never quite is.
You've reflected on how you responded and what you gave. One more lens — the widest one. Let's look at who else was in your day.
When you think about today's biggest challenge or moment of stress — who came to mind?
options: Mainly me — it was my problem to carry
My immediate team — we were all in it
A specific colleague who had it harder than I did
The people we ultimately serve — customers, users, students

match: Mainly me — it was my problem to carry
My immediate team — we were all in it
A specific colleague who had it harder than I did
The people we ultimately serve — customers, users, students

nodes.text: That's honest. Now expand the frame a little — was there anyone else affected by what you were going through today, even if you didn't interact with them directly?
options: Yes — I can see now how my stress rippled outward
Probably — I wasn't paying attention to that in the moment
Maybe, but their experience felt secondary to me
Not that I'm aware of — my situation felt self-contained

nodes.text: You noticed others in the mix. When you think about that person or group — did you do anything with that awareness?
options: Yes — I checked in or adjusted my behavior because of them
I noticed but didn't act on it — it stayed as awareness
I wanted to help but I didn't know how
I acknowledged it mentally but was too absorbed in my own situation

match: Yes — I can see now how my stress rippled outward
Probably — I wasn't paying attention to that in the moment
Maybe, but their experience felt secondary to me
Not that I'm aware of — my situation felt self-contained
Yes — I checked in or adjusted my behavior because of them
I noticed but didn't act on it — it stayed as awareness
I wanted to help but I didn't know how
I acknowledged it mentally but was too absorbed in my own situation

nodes.text: Noticing others and then *acting on that noticing* — that's the full loop. Awareness that doesn't change behavior is just observation. You closed the loop today. That's harder than it sounds when you're in the middle of your own demands.

You saw beyond yourself today — even if you didn't act on it. Awareness is the first step and it isn't automatic. Tomorrow: one moment where you let that awareness become a gesture. It doesn't have to be large. It just has to be real.

You started the day inside your own experience — and you're ending it with a wider lens. That expansion matters. The fact that you can now see how your situation touched others is a form of emotional intelligence most people skip entirely when they're under stress.

A day that felt self-contained is often more connected than it looked. It's not a flaw to be absorbed in your own experience — it's just a habit. The practice is widening the aperture: not to take on everyone's problems, but to notice that you exist inside a web of other people who also had a day today.

Today in one picture:

On agency — you leaned {axis1.dominant}. You described today as "{A1_OPEN.answer}" and when pressed, you {axis1.summary_phrase}.

On contribution — you leaned {axis2.dominant}. {axis2.summary_phrase}.

On your radius of concern — you leaned {axis3.dominant}. {axis3.summary_phrase}.

One thing worth carrying into tomorrow: {closing_prompt}

internal.label: toward ownership
internal.summary_phrase: you located your hand in the outcome

external.label: toward circumstance
external.summary_phrase: you felt more pulled by external forces than your own choices

contribution.label: toward giving
contribution.summary_phrase: What you gave today shaped the space around you, whether or not it was noticed.

entitlement.label: toward receiving
entitlement.summary_phrase: You were tracking what you were owed — worth noticing, not judging.

altrocentric.label: outward
altrocentric.summary_phrase: You held others in your awareness today.

selfcentric.label: inward
selfcentric.summary_phrase: Today's frame was mostly your own experience — a valid starting point.

closing_prompts.internal_contribution_altrocentric: You showed up well today. The question for tomorrow is whether you can do it more deliberately.
closing_prompts.internal_contribution_selfcentric: You acted with agency and gave generously — now try widening who that generosity reaches.
closing_prompts.internal_entitlement_altrocentric: You see your agency and you see others — bridge those two into contribution tomorrow.
closing_prompts.internal_entitlement_selfcentric: You know what you can control. Tomorrow, ask: what can I give — not what do I deserve?
closing_prompts.external_contribution_altrocentric: You gave to others even when the day felt outside your hands. That's a form of strength.
closing_prompts.external_contribution_selfcentric: You contributed despite the difficulty. Widen the circle — who else was in your day today?
closing_prompts.external_entitlement_altrocentric: A hard day and you still noticed others. That awareness — build on it with one small action tomorrow.
closing_prompts.external_entitlement_selfcentric: Tomorrow: just one moment where you find your agency, and one moment where you give without expecting anything back.

nodes.text: Rest well. You showed up enough today just by looking at it honestly. See you tomorrow.
